DEMURRER to 2ND Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jan-13-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearance is made in this matter.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling.

Defendant City and County of San Francisco's demurrer to the second amended complaint is overruled. A demurrer may only be brought on the grounds set forth in Code of Civil Procedure section 430.10. "A demurrer shall distinctly specify the grounds upon (which any of the objections to the complaint, cross-complaint, or answer are taken. Unless it does so, it may be disregarded." (Code Civ. Proc. sec. 430.60.) Defendant does not specify any statutory ground for demurring to the claims asserted in the second amended complaint on behalf of the individually named defendants.

In any event, the City's arguments on the merits are unpersuasive. First, the City contends that the Court "should not allow" Plaintiffs to name individual defendants because it would be "redundant and unnecessary." Even under the City's own authority, however, that does not constitute grounds for dismissing those claims, which state sufficient facts to constitute a cause of action against the individual defendants in their official capacity. (See Hoffmann v. City and County of San Francisco (N.D. Cal. 2012) 870 F.Supp.2d 799, 803 [acknowledging that suits may be brought against government officials acting in their official or personal capacities, and granting plaintiffs leave to amend section 1983 claim against City and official capacity defendants].) The City's Monell argument concerns Plaintiffs' claims against the City, not against the individual defendants.

The Court is also unpersuaded by the City's second argument, that the Court "should not allow" Plaintiffs to name individual defendants under Labor Code section 1102.5 because "adding individual defendants to the section 1102.5 claim would not add anything substantive to the lawsuit." Labor Code section 1102.5 provides in relevant part that "[a]n employer, or any person acting on behalf of the employer, shall not retaliate against an employee for disclosing information . . . ." (Lab. Code sec. 1102.5(b) (emphasis added).) Thus, the plain statutory language makes clear that an individual may be held liable for violating section 1102.5. The City's position would require the Court to ignore the emphasized language, which is inconsistent with standard canons of statutory interpretation that require courts to "accord[] significance, if possible, to every word, phrase and sentence in pursuance of the legislative purpose," and warn that "[a] construction making some words surplusage is to be avoided." (Dyna-Med, Inc. v. Fair Employment & Housing Com. (1987) 43 Cal.3d 1379, 1387.) For that reason, the Court declines to follow the contrary holdings of certain federal district courts, which are not binding on it.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the adopted ruling.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: J. Rios Merida; Reporter: proceeding not reported.
